slides.title: What Are Dunder Methods?
slides.body: **Dunder methods** (double-underscore, like `__str__`) are special hooks Python calls automatically when you use built-in operations. Define them on your class and your objects behave just like Python's own built-ins.

slides.title: Make print() Work Your Way
slides.body: Without `__str__`, printing your object shows an ugly memory address. Define `__str__` and Python calls it whenever someone does `print(obj)` — it must **return a string**.

slides.title: __repr__: The Developer View
slides.body: `__repr__` is the **developer-facing** representation — it should look like code you'd write to recreate the object. It also acts as a fallback when `__str__` is not defined.
slides.tip: Rule of thumb: always define __repr__ — it doubles as __str__ when needed

slides.title: Operator Overloading with __add__
slides.body: Define `__add__` and the `+` operator works on your objects. Always **return a new object** — never modify `self` in place.
slides.tip: Other operators: __eq__ for ==, __len__ for len(), __lt__ for <

slides.title: You've Got the Magic!
slides.body: Dunder methods let your classes **plug in to Python's own syntax** — `print()`, `len()`, `+`, `==`, and more. You're not hacking the language; you're using exactly the same hooks the built-ins use.
slides.tip: Start with __repr__, add __str__, then layer in operators as you need them
